Neural Architecture Search for Edge Devices

Abstract

We present an efficient neural architecture search method optimized for
deployment on edge devices with limited computational resources.

1 Introduction

Edge computing requires models that balance accuracy with inference speed.
Figure 1. Overview of our NAS pipeline showing the search space, controller,
and evaluation stages. The controller generates candidate architectures that
are evaluated on a proxy task before full training.

Our approach builds on differentiable NAS methods but introduces novel
constraints for memory-bounded search. Fig. 2 illustrates the memory
profiling component that guides architecture selection.

2 Methods

The search space includes standard convolution, depthwise separable
convolution, and squeeze-and-excite blocks. Table 1. Search space
dimensions and ranges for each operation type. We define the search
space as a directed acyclic graph with variable depth.

Figure 3: Comparison of search efficiency between our method and baseline
approaches. Our method converges 3.5x faster while finding architectures
with comparable accuracy. Tab. 1 summarizes the latency measurements.

3 Results

Our discovered architectures achieve 76.3% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet
while requiring only 2.1M parameters. Figure 4. Pareto frontier of
accuracy vs. latency for discovered architectures compared to manually
designed networks. Table 2: Detailed accuracy comparison across different
hardware platforms including Raspberry Pi 4, Jetson Nano, and Pixel 6.

4 Conclusion

We have demonstrated that hardware-aware NAS can discover efficient
architectures for edge deployment. Fig. 5 shows deployment results.
